input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan with all commands.

---

## .NET MAUI Setup on Windows 11 (Android + Windows)

### 1. Install .NET SDK

```powershell
# Check if .NET is already installed
dotnet --list-sdks

# Install via winget (discovers latest stable .NET 9 SDK)
winget search Microsoft.DotNet.SDK
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9 --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements

# Refresh PATH (or restart terminal)
$env:Path = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "Machine") + ";" + [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "User")

# Verify
dotnet --version
```

### 2. Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to confirm naming
dotnet workload search maui

# Install MAUI (pulls in android, ios stubs, etc.)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

### 3. Install Android SDK & Dependencies

The MAUI workload installs Android SDK components automatically, but you may need to manage them. The SDK lands here by default:

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was provisioned
$androidSdk = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA "Microsoft\Android\sdk"
if (!(Test-Path $androidSdk)) {
    # Fallback: check dotnet's android provisioning
    $androidSdk = Join-Path $env:USERPROFILE ".android\sdk"
}
Write-Host "Android SDK location: $androidSdk"

# Set ANDROID_HOME so tooling can find it
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME", $androidSdk, "User")
$env:ANDROID_HOME = $androidSdk
```

**If the SDK is missing or incomplete**, install it manually:

```powershell
# Install Android Studio via winget (includes SDK Manager)
winget search Google.AndroidStudio
winget install Google.AndroidStudio --accept-source-agreements

# OR use the standalone command-line tools:
# https://developer.android.com/studio#command-line-tools-only
# Then use sdkmanager to install what MAUI needs (see step 4)
```

### 4. Ensure Correct Android SDK Components

```powershell
# Discover what API levels / build-tools are available
$sdkmanager = Join-Path $env:ANDROID_HOME "cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat"

# List installed packages
& $sdkmanager --list_installed

# List all available packages (grep for what you need)
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "platforms;android-" | Select-Object -First 10
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "build-tools;"    | Select-Object -First 10
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "ndk;"             | Select-Object -First 5

# Discover what MAUI actually requires — check the workload manifest:
dotnet workload update --print-download-urls 2>&1 | Select-String "android"

# Typical install (adjust API level to match MAUI's TargetFramework, e.g. net9.0-android35):
& $sdkmanager "platforms;android-35" "build-tools;35.0.0" "platform-tools" "emulator"
& $sdkmanager --licenses  # Accept all licenses
```

### 5. Set Up an Android Emulator

```powershell
# List available system images
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "system-images;android-35"

# Install a system image
& $sdkmanager "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Create an AVD
$avdmanager = Join-Path $env:ANDROID_HOME "cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat"
& $avdmanager create avd -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" -d "pixel_7"

# Verify
& $avdmanager list avd
```

### 6. Enable Windows App SDK Target (for WinUI 3)

```powershell
# Windows App SDK comes with the MAUI workload, but ensure Developer Mode is on:
Start-Process "ms-settings:developers"
# → Toggle "Developer Mode" ON in the window that opens

# Verify Windows targeting support
dotnet new list | Select-String "maui"
```

### 7. Create and Build a Test Project

```powershell
# Scaffold a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n MauiTestApp
cd MauiTestApp

# Discover available target frameworks in the project
Select-String 'TargetFrameworks' *.csproj

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Build for Windows
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (start emulator first)
$emulator = Join-Path $env:ANDROID_HOME "emulator\emulator.exe"
Start-Process $emulator -ArgumentList "-avd Pixel_MAUI" -NoNewWindow
dotnet build -f net9.0-android -t:Run
```

### 8. Troubleshooting & Diagnostics

```powershell
# Full environment diagnostic
dotnet workload list
dotnet --info

# Check for workload updates
dotnet workload update

# If Java JDK is missing (Android builds need it):
winget search Microsoft.OpenJDK
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17

# Set JAVA_HOME
$javaHome = (Get-ChildItem "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\jdk-*" | Sort-Object Name -Descending | Select-Object -First 1).FullName
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME", $javaHome, "User")
$env:JAVA_HOME = $javaHome
Write-Host "JAVA_HOME set to $javaHome"
```

---

**Key version-discovery tips:**
- `dotnet workload search` and `sdkmanager --list` are your friends for finding current versions dynamically
- The `TargetFrameworks` line in the `.csproj` tells you which Android API level to install
- Always run `dotnet workload update` before starting to get the latest compatible components

The user wants a complete step-by-step plan for setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11, targeting Android and Windows. They can't run commands now but want all the PowerShell commands ready. Let me provide a comprehensive guide.

Let me think about what's needed:

1. Install .NET SDK (latest .NET 8 or .NET 9)
2. Install .NET MAUI workload
3. Install Android SDK, NDK, emulator
4. Install Visual Studio or VS Code (optional but recommended)
5. Set up environment variables
6. Verify everything works
